steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student concludes that similarity drives cross-category purchasing of the same brand. The question asks which finding would most directly weaken that causal claim. To weaken a causal conclusion, we need an alternative explanation for the observed correlation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Products in pairs with high similarity ratings tended to be among the most expensive products in their categories, whereas products in pairs with low similarity ratings tended to be among the least expensive products in their categories.": Price difference between pairs does not explain why cross-category purchasing correlates with similarity. Irrelevant to the causal claim. ✗
  • "Consumers were more likely to purchase both products in pairs with similarity ratings comparable to those of bread and bagels than they were to purchase both products in pairs with similarity ratings comparable to those of deodorant and baking soda.": This simply restates the original data the student used — it supports rather than weakens the conclusion. ✗
  • "Pairs with high similarity ratings tended to be in categories in which there were relatively few competing products from other brands, whereas pairs with low similarity ratings tended to be in categories in which there were many competing products from other brands.": This introduces a confounding variable — limited competition, not similarity itself, could drive cross-category buying. If fewer rival brands are available, consumers naturally buy both items from the same brand. This alternative explanation undermines the student's causal claim. ✓
  • "The positive correlation between similarity ratings and cross-category purchasing was equally strong for pairs with low brand recognition and pairs with high brand recognition.": This shows the similarity effect persists regardless of brand recognition, which strengthens (not weakens) the idea that similarity is the key driver. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
